item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this article; it's an incredibly creative concept and the pacing is excellent. Also, shoutout to including indigenous Mexican civilizations in the addenda. I actually didn't mind the fact that it's a miniature earth being left unexplained/unexplored (just adding to the weirdness is enough for me), but the people who brought that up have a valid point. In response to Doctor_Sykes's criticism about it not being noticed, I think that its size (38mm) is an adequate explanation.

Some questions/criticism:

  • How did the Foundation predict the sphere's height before the first test? 'Distance from the sun' isn't the first factor most people would think of in this scenario, and even if they knew that they wouldn't have known the proportionality constant.
  • Why did they switch to D-class after it didn't hit the goat? They could have just put another animal in its path and made sure no soil was in the way.
  • "Change velocity at a 90-degree angle" is somewhat ambiguous; did it suddenly change its velocity to be straight up or did it just accelerate straight up while maintaining a constant horizontal velocity?
  • Why didn't the sphere avoid the soil in Test Log A? The steel plates were close enough to shatter the glass, so clearly they were within the 45-50m range at which the sphere started trying to avoid the soil in Log B.
  • In Log C, "velocity" should be "speed".
  • The Russian infinite-energy generator is interesting, and I like the concept, but the possibility of there being another 3958 should either be expanded upon or removed. An easy edit would be to just move it to the middle latitudes.
  • Addendum NT-1 says "the reason NT-1 is of such note is its location"; that's certainly one reason it's of note, but even if it were located somewhere that 3958 can normally get to, it would still be noteworthy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the bits about how people pf different cultures come up with myths and reasoning about what the thing is actually about. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, after reading through this, I definitely like the idea of an "unstoppable object" with a fixed position relative to another object, and the idea of having it follow the sun was also interesting. However, the last few addenda, including the ending "explanation," seem a bit forced and unrealistically wacky, (like the article goes from an in-depth explanation of the trajectory of the object to a short paragraph about a guy who apparently invented infinite power somehow) which disturbs the tone of the article.

The fact that only a few ancient people knew about it, especially when it gets as close as 1.5 meters to the ground, also suspends disbelief a little, and there's no anomalous explanation for that. Plus, as others have mentioned, the fact that the object is a tiny replica of Earth seems a little arbitrary. In any case, while it's clear you put a lot of thought into the science of the object, the attempts at contextualizing the object in history and explaining its origins, as well as the fact that it is basically uncontainable and yet is not very often seen ruined it a bit for me.

I want to up-vote for such a creative idea, but the execution has forced me to a no-vote (it might just be my tastes but still).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My biggest gripe is the last document log. To me it seems like your trying to explain it too much.

I don't really know how to fix it completely, but I suggest rearranging the sentences to make the reader figure out that there could be another 3958 by themselves instead of it being spoon fed to them. No vote for now, might change if this is fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the most interesting things about this article that doesn't get touched on at all is that it's a replica of the planet Earth. I want to know what's it's like on that Earth: are there people living there that have to deal with their world constantly punching through stuff? Is there no life at all? Is it an exact replica, with it's own SCP-3958?

My first impression reading this article was: "Oh, a miniature replica of Earth. I wonder where he takes that," but the the article was just about the science of where it's located. Now, that was done very well but I don't see the reason why it's a replica of Earth (or how the Foundation really managed to figure that out) instead of just being anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first thought
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you tell me what's unclear to you? I definitely want to improve it. I'll also look into trimming the Test Log's fat where possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea here but the execution is kind of sloppy. The descriptions aren't super clear in a lot of places, to the point where I was getting lost as to what exactly was going on. The test logs are also pretty long considering how little new information they convey.

There's good stuff in here, but I need a more clear presentation to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
According to some other site, 60 days after perihelion would put the Earth about a quarter way back to aphelion distance. But depending on the monument's latitude, it could be less than 60 days. I think the great pyramid is too far north to ever get hit, but further south gets hit earlier/lower.

But who's to say it wasn't an anomalously tall monument, anomalously far south :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The monument was only a 150 m high. I'm afraid I messed up some calculations there. My bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, for fun…

On the average, one revolution of the Earth relative to perihelion is about 25 minutes longer than one revolution relative to the December solstice. Perihelion advances one full calendar date every 60 or so years.

During the late Mexica period, it would have fallen a few days after solstice, placing the sphere clearly in that area.

In the 1st or 2nd century BC, it would have fallen in the first few days of December, probably right near where you have it in India.

However, 4500 years ago, perihelion would have fallen about 61 days before December solstice (equivalent of October 21st if there had been such a thing as October back then). Which would have put the sphere more around… 10.5-11° N according to a calculator I found.

South Sudan, or Nicaragua, or Vietnam… I'm not sure, any place that officially had antechambers or hieroglyphics. Unless that monument was somewhere over 400m tall (I dunno exactly) it wasn't getting knocked off… Unless you handwave it as "hieroglyphics and antechambers the public doesn't know about", in which case they could also be anomalously tall :p

Interestingly (to me at least) the sidereal year (revolution around the sun relative to fixed stars) is about 21 minutes longer than the tropical year (time between solstices/equinoxes) and about 4 minutes shorter than the anomalistic year (time between perihelions/aphelions).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me quite a bit of the White Cow Moon (a mysterious low-orbiting satellite with anomalous properties whose existence is a heavily guarded secret) in Gene Wolfe's "Has Anybody Seen Junie Moon?". Doesn't affect my upvote one bit though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both excellent ideas. Thank you very much. Will edit ASAP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I see.

So, my 2 suggestions then. The first one is I think absolutely necessary… The second would have clarified it for me.

1. Change "between summer and winter" to "between July and January". The first one just doesn't make sense, and that's jarring. This sphere never appears in a place where it's summer. In July, it's zooming 2500m over Australian winter.

2. Either as a footnote on dependent or an extra sentence around there, maybe "the changes in altitude of the sphere relative to the center of the Earth are directly proportional to the change in distance from the Earth to the Sun". But you can probably write it more clearly than that :) No need for the math, just explain the relationship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're actually spot on. Its distance to the earth is proportionate to the distance between Earth and Sun. The change in height is proportional: Earth is, at its closest, 147,100,993km away from the Sun. Then, it moves away by, roughly, 5,000,000km. I used the relationship between those, and extrapolated that to the center of the earth to the position of the sphere. 0.03% of the distance between the center of the earth and the position of the sphere is roughly 2500 meters.

Do you think the article would benefit from the calculation? Some of the people I talked to figured it would not be interesting for most readers, as it doesn't add much. But the downside is that we do end up here, where you - and probably others - might be unclear on how its height/distance works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hovers at altitudes between 1.5 and 2860 meters above sea level (dependent on the changes in distance from the Earth to the Sun between summer and winter.)

If I'm completely misunderstanding what you mean here let me know.

First, the Earth is closest to the sun in January and farthest in July. It has nothing to do with winter and summer. In January, this object would be in the northern hemisphere (where it's winter) and in July, it would be in the southern hemisphere (where it's winter). And in between, it would get closer to the equator.

Second, you write that it's observable in India in the winter, so… The sphere gets closer to the Earth as the Earth gets closer to the sun? Is that your intent? Because the opposite makes a little more sense to me… But then you'd be seeing it close to Earth in South America, southern Africa, and Australia, instead of Mexico and India. Today. These change over time, but I'm not sure whether they change relative to the axial tilt, and if so over what timescale. The point is, if the distance between this object and the Earth varies related to the distance between the sun and the Earth, spell out which way it's related, and maybe double check things were the same 4500 years ago…

Third, the distance between earth and the sun varies by 5 million kilometres between January and July, not 2500 metres. Just putting that out there, like I said you didn't spell out the relationship.

If there's a gap in my understanding of this article (or of Lagrange points which I think I understand and Earth's orbital parameters which I literally just googled) lemme know.
